chunk_summary: This section discusses screening and initial management of obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) in children with metabolic risk, emphasizing routine screening, diagnostic guidelines, and management strategies including adenotonsillectomy and weight loss.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of obstructive sleep apnea on cardiometabolic risk in children.
Learn the recommended screening criteria and diagnostic approaches for OSA in pediatric metabolic risk clinics.
Identify first-line management strategies for OSA, including adenotonsillectomy and weight loss.
Recognize practical constraints in low-resource settings and prioritize risk-based referrals.
key_facts: Sleep-disordered breathing worsens insulin resistance, increases blood pressure, and negatively affects liver health.
Routine screening for OSA includes symptoms such as snoring ≥3 nights/week, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness, and morning headaches.
The 2012 AAP guideline recommends overnight polysomnography (PSG) for children with snoring and OSA symptoms.
Adenotonsillectomy is the first-line therapy for OSA when adenotonsillar hypertrophy is present.
Weight loss is recommended for children with OSA and obesity to improve outcomes.
In low-access settings, prioritize referrals for children with significant symptoms, hypertension, or severe obesity.
reference_quote: The 2012 AAP guideline recommends overnight polysomnography (PSG) for children who snore and have symptoms or signs of OSA. When PSG is unavailable, alternative testing or referral to a specialist is recommended rather than dismissing symptoms. (Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22926173/)
document_key_topics: Impact of obstructive sleep apnea on metabolic risk in children
Screening criteria and diagnostic guidelines for OSA
Management strategies for OSA, including adenotonsillectomy and weight loss
Challenges in low-resource settings for OSA diagnosis and treatment
